q: All fluids are considered to be only liquids.
The volume of a gas is independent of both pressure and temperature.
Boyle's law and Charles' law are relevant to the volume of a liquid.
The volume of a liquid always depends on pressure, even if its temperature is constant.
An incompressible liquid is one whose density remains constant for any pressure, provided that the temperature is kept constant.
The path or flow of fluid particles is referred to as a streamline.
Motion of fluids is classified into three main types: laminar, turbulent, and transitional flow.
In laminar flow, fluid particles move irregularly.
Laminar flow is characterized by layers of fluid mixing with adjacent layers.
Streamlines in a steady laminar flow frequently cross each other.
In laminar flow, every fluid particle arriving at a given point has a different velocity.
Steady flow implies that fluid particles move in smooth paths without mixing.
Turbulent flow occurs when fluid particles move in smooth and predictable paths.
In turbulent flow, the direction of fluid particles remains constant.
The speed of the fluid at a point in turbulent flow changes only in magnitude, not direction.
Whirling fluid movement is a characteristic of laminar flow.
The speed of fluid in turbulent flow is constant at any given point.
Ideal laminar fluid flow assumes that the fluid is compressible.
In ideal laminar fluid flow, internal friction is a significant factor.
Viscosity is the term used for internal friction in fluids.
Ideal laminar fluid flow considers a high viscosity.
Turbulent flow is taken into account when considering ideal laminar fluid flow.
Sea breeze is presented as an example of turbulent flow.
A storm is an example of a laminar fluid flow.
